articles.title: The Falling Regimes
articles.content:
伊朗政权的垮台来如风暴，但这实际上是限制大陆另一端更大冲突的更广泛战略的延伸。在对委内瑞拉能源出口实施有效控制后，中国盟国剩余的能源基础设施来源仅剩伊朗和俄罗斯。通过政治手段将印度拉近西方阵营，使印度回归其作为民主国家与民主国家联盟的自然状态，从而切断俄罗斯的战费来源——美国使出了最大杠杆之一，因为俄罗斯对印贸易是俄罗斯经济最大的资金来源之一。美国正试图扼杀其对手继续乌克兰冲突的能力，而取代俄罗斯石油收入始终是实现这一目标的唯一最佳途径。

挑战伊朗在波斯湾地区的霸权，与限制俄罗斯与印度广泛贸易的做法如出一辙——夺取伊朗能源出口的控制权是关键。伊朗大部分能源供应中国，委内瑞拉和伊朗都是中国经济的关键能源来源。虽然意图并非切断中国能源供应，但通过西方控制的石油和天然气来源控制对中国的能源供应，将使能源制裁能够迅速回应中国若选择以武力威胁台湾及西方新盟友印度时的安全平衡。

确保伊朗成为盟友的方式可能有多种。西班牙佛朗哥政权于七十年代末在长期独裁者去世后终结，权力移交给胡安·卡洛斯二世国王。这位由佛朗哥指定维护政权的国王，决定运用其主权将西班牙转变为民主国家。伊朗王储礼萨·帕拉维可以以西班牙为典范——如果西班牙最终愿意支持一个自由的伊朗。

伊朗境内有几个少数民族群体，依靠他们最好服务于各自的本地利益。其中许多群体，如伊朗和伊拉克的库尔德社区，遭受过伊朗政权的严重暴力迫害，若获得武器，他们可能寻求独立。在他们支持打击ISIS以及成为解放其他伊朗人民的武装力量之际，当他们成为对抗该地区每个西方对手的先锋时，忽视他们自己的建国抱负是不恰当的。若与北约成员国发生热战，即使伊朗在新政府领导下恢复稳定，西方盟友也将陷入库尔德社区相关冲突的两难境地。

目前尚不清楚伊朗主要城市的伊朗自由抵抗力量究竟是谁——这是伊朗最终摆脱现政权的关键需求。若没有独立、本土的团体能够在地面上接管自由伊朗政府，外部力量可能不会为另一个国家流血破财，除非有重大收益。正如彼得·奥图尔在《阿拉伯的劳伦斯》中所言：“带走英国工程师，你就带走了英国政府”——这是波斯和伊朗过去几代人熟知的道理。伊朗本地人实现自由目标的窗口期可能有限，因为在全力支持伊朗自由的西方大国中，存在大量对手，这些对手强烈反对西方任何解放伊朗的行动，至少不会公开支持政权及其盟友。具有讽刺意味的是，几个世纪以来在伊朗政权更迭中发挥重要作用的英国，在2026年对伊朗人争取自由的诉求表现出几乎毫无支持。遗憾的是，西方许多政府历史上对每一次伊朗自由起义都选择无视。

伊朗真正变革的窗口即将关闭，而反对伊朗自由联盟的计划已经在运转。当和平抗议者在街头被屠杀时，美国盟友正采取措施将自己与支持伊朗导弹力量的国家拉近——这些导弹如今正在德黑兰以西的每个国家造成暴力冲击。虽然北美能源可以通过在冲突爆发时控制供应来稳定中国经济，但一些西方盟友似乎并不完全支持中东新民主国家的建立。尽管专家警告美国盟友不要向伊朗政权盟友靠拢，但抵制美国的坚持超出了对他们自己社区的支持——现实情况是，据悉伊朗特工距美国边境仅一两个小时车程，甚至可能目前已在美国境内。面对这样的现实，国土安全部的资金将无法及时到位。伊朗人需要尽快解放自己，因为各方力量已经在联合起来终结他们获得自由的机会。从伊朗抗议者多年来被忽视的方式来看，他们很清楚自己盟友的对手，即使在西方也是如此。
articles.summary:
The dismantling of the Iranian Regime came in like a storm, but it is really an extension of a greater strategy to limit a larger conflict at the other end of the continent. With the securing of a significant level of control over Venezuelan energy exports, the remaining source of energy infrastructure from China’s allies resided in Iran and Russia. With the political move to cut off Russia’s war funding by placing India closer to the West and putting India in its natural state of a democracy linked to other democracies, the United States pulled one of its biggest levers as Russia’s dependence on trade with India is one of the greatest sources of funding for the Russian economy. The United States is seeking to strangle the ability for its adversaries to continue the conflict in Ukraine, and displacing Russian oil revenues was always the only best way to achieve this aim.

Challenging Iran’s hegemony in the Persian Gulf region comes with a similarities in limiting Russia’s extensive trade with India, mirrored by taking control of Iran’s energy exports, most of which are in supply of China, with Venezuela and Iran being key sources of energy for China’s economy. While the intent is not to cut off China from energy to its economy, controlling the energy supply to China via Western controlled sources of oil and gas would enable energy sanctions to quickly respond to aggressive actions if China chose to intimidate the security balance with Taiwan, and the West’s new significant ally, India.

A move to secure Iran as an ally may come in several forms. The end of the Franco regime in Spain in the late 70s came after the passing of the long time Dictator, to which control was given to King Juan Carlos II. The King, who was directed by Franco to maintain the regime, decided to use his sovereign power to turn Spain into a democracy, a path Crown Prince Pahlavi of Iran could follow with great example from Spain, if Spain would consider finally supporting a free Iran.

While there are a few minority ethnic groups inside of Iran, depending on them would do best to serve their own local interests. Many of these groups, like the Kurdish community in Iran and in Iraq, suffered significant violence from Iran’s regime, and may seek independence if given the swords and rifles to free the region. With their support against ISIS as well as being the force to liberate other Iranian nationals, it would be inappropriate to ignore their own ambitions for nationhood when they were the tip of the spear against every Western adversary in the region. A hot conflict with NATO members would place Western allies between sides in the conflict involving the Kurdish community, even if Iran was able to stabilize under a free Government.

It is unclear who the Iranian free resistance is on the ground in Iran’s major cities, a crucial necessity if Iran will eventually free itself from the current regime. Without an independent and indigenous group able to take over the Government for a Free Iran on the ground, outside forces will likely not spill blood and treasure for the sake of another nation without significant gains. As Peter O’Toole states in his role as Lawrence of Arabia, “Take English engineers and you take English government”, a relationship well known by Persians and Iranians of the past generations. There is likely a limited window for local Iranians to achieve their goals of freedom, as in Western nations in full support of Iran’s freedom, there are a great number of adversaries and they are aggressively against any actions by the West to free Iran, if not openly in support of the regime and their allies. Ironically, the British who have had such a great hand in regimes in Iran for generations have openly shown little support for actual Iranians wishing to free themselves in 2026. Unfortunately, many Governments in the West have made history by ignoring every uprising for a Free Iran without fail.

The window will close soon on real change in Iran, and plans are already circling the against the alliance to Free Iranians. While peaceful protesters were being massacred in the streets, US allies were making moves to bring themselves closer to countries supporting Iran’s missile force, now violently impacting every country West of Tehran. While North American energy could be a source to stabilize China’s economy by keeping a hold on supplies in the event of a hot conflict, it seems unlikely that some Western allies are in full support of a new democracy in the Middle East. Even with experts warning US allies from moving to close to Iran’s regime allies, the insistence to resist the United States goes beyond support for their own communities, placing the reality that Iranian agents are now known to be emanating just an hour or two from the US border, if not residing inside the United States currently. With such realities, Homeland Security will not be funded soon enough. Iranians need to free themselves as soon as possible, as forces are already combining to end their opportunity for freedom. From the way Iranian protesters have been ignored for years, they are well aware of the adversaries of their allies, even in the West.

articles.title: Measuring Sovereignty in an Age of Strategic Illusions
articles.content:
新版美国《国家安全战略》以前所未有的清晰度阐述了主权语言。它援引西半球的“关键地形”概念，重新诠释半球主义学说，减少对欧洲的安全承诺，并发出选择性接触的转向信号。这是一份不以普世自由秩序为中心，而是以国家自主、战略控制和力量投射为核心的战略。然而，这种表面上的清晰之下存在结构性弱点：华盛顿仍然缺乏一套连贯的体系来衡量主权本身。

传统指标——国内生产总值、国防预算、军队规模——只能捕捉规模，而非自主性。一个国家可能拥有全球最大军事力量，却仍依赖外国供应链。它可能主导技术市场，却遭受教育衰退，削弱长期创新能力。它可能享有全球文化影响力，却面临国内分裂，削弱政治决策能力。2026年的主权是多维度的。不同时衡量这些维度，战略就只能是愿景而非行动纲领。

伯克主权指数由国际伯克研究所开发，正是为了解决这一测量缺口。它从七个维度评估国家自主性：政治、经济、技术、信息、文化、认知和军事主权。每个维度采用0至100分制评分，综合使用联合国、世界银行、国际货币基金组织、教科文组织、斯德哥尔摩国际和平研究所、PISA及其他机构的官方国际数据，并结合来自50多个国家、每个组成部分100多名专家的校准评估。最终得分——最高700分——代表统计指标与专家评估的算术平均值。

2024-2025年的结果令人警醒。美国得分650.9分（满分700分）。中国得分649.1分。差距：1.8分——在总分框架内差异不足0.3%。对于两个被广泛认为处于不同战略层级的国家而言，这种统计上的接近应该从根本上重塑华盛顿的辩论。

美国仍保有明显优势。军事主权得分为96.0，反映了9620亿美元的国防预算、约5400枚核弹头以及无与伦比的全球部署能力。技术主权得分为95.4，得益于研发支出占GDP的3.4%-3.6%，以及在人工智能、生物技术和微电子领域的领先地位。然而，其他维度上出现了结构性脆弱。

政治主权得分为87.8，反映了极化、反复出现的政府停摆和公众信任度下降。认知主权——尽管总体得分高达95.4——包含警示信号：成人功能性识字率在79%-81%之间波动，美国PISA数学成绩为469分，低于经合组织平均水平。工业自主性仍部分暴露：约30%的先进微电子元件依赖进口。

中国的概况在结构上有所不同。军事主权得分为94.5，技术主权为91.6——略低于美国。但政治主权达到90.8，反映了集中决策和高度的制度凝聚力。信息主权得分为93.2，由服务11亿用户的封闭国家数字生态系统支撑，不受西方平台渗透。文化主权达到95.1，得益于60处联合国教科文组织遗产地和6800多家博物馆。

最重要的是经济效率。中国在人均财富仅为美国三分之一至二分之一的情况下，接近实现对等。中国的购买力平价人均GDP在25000-30500美元之间，而美国为76800-89100美元。这一趋同反映了协调的跨维度投资：教育扩展至高等教育入学率60.8%，研发支出占GDP的2.68%（绝对值约5060亿美元），以及“中国制造2025”下的长期产业战略。对等并非通过单一领域的主导实现，而是通过七个维度的同步发展达成。

这一多维视角重新诠释了新《国防战略》中的若干假设。首先，重建美国国防工业基础不能仅靠军事拨款。工业主权需要经济资本、教育能力、技术独立性和政治稳定性的协调配合。美国目前公共债务占GDP的119%-124%，国债超过36-41万亿美元，教育不平等加剧，党派分裂深刻。工厂可以获得资助；但全面的国家动员需要社会凝聚力。

其次，盟友分担负担产生了战略悖论。欧洲北约国家合计GDP是俄罗斯的13倍，但装备本土化仍然有限。立陶宛国防支出占GDP的4%-6%，但约85%的装备依赖进口。相比之下，土耳其——尽管支出比例较低——在国防生产中实现了约70%的本土化，包括国产无人机系统。真正的 sovereignty 增加战略自主性。自主性降低可预测性。

第三，中东伙伴正从依赖悄然转向能力建设。沙特的“2030愿景”包括本土工业化、网络安全基础设施和技术转让。一旦建立的主权往往会改变联盟行为。具有能力的盟友本质上会独立行事。

这一令人不安的含义很清楚：华盛顿的战略要求 sovereignty——在国内和盟友中——却缺乏一套全面的仪表盘来衡量 sovereignty 是在被建设还是在被侵蚀。

伯克框架并不预测冲突或崩溃。它衡量的是能力，而非意图。但它揭示了传统权力指标无法看到结构性动态。它迫使同时评估教育、技术、凝聚力、信息控制、工业韧性和治理。

美国仍略微领先。但在700分制中领先1.8分并非结构性优势。这是竞争均衡。2026年的 sovereignty 不是由拥有最大军事力量或最多联盟来定义。它是由在压力下维持多领域独立行动的能力来定义。这需要教育复兴、工业自主、政治稳定和技术独立协调运作——而非各自为政。

新版《国防战略》确定了正确的优先事项：sovereignty。它所缺乏的是衡量向该目标进展的系统性机制。没有衡量，sovereignty 就沦为修辞。有了衡量，它就成为战略。
articles.summary:
The new American National Defense Strategy speaks the language of sovereignty with unusual clarity. It invokes “key terrain” in the Western Hemisphere, reframes hemispheric doctrine, reduces security guarantees to Europe, and signals a shift toward selective engagement. It is a strategy centered not on universal liberal order, but on national autonomy, strategic control, and power projection. Yet beneath this rhetorical clarity lies a structural weakness: Washington still lacks a coherent system for measuring sovereignty itself.

Traditional metrics—GDP, defense budgets, force size—capture scale, but not autonomy. A state may command the world’s largest military yet remain dependent on foreign supply chains. It may dominate technology markets yet suffer educational decline that undermines long-term innovation. It may enjoy global cultural influence while experiencing domestic fragmentation that weakens political decision-making capacity. Sovereignty in 2026 is multidimensional. Without measuring those dimensions simultaneously, strategy becomes aspirational rather than operational.

The Burke Sovereignty Index, developed by the International Burke Institute, addresses precisely this measurement gap. It evaluates national autonomy across seven dimensions: political, economic, technological, informational, cultural, cognitive, and military sovereignty. Each dimension is scored from 0 to 100 using official international data (UN, World Bank, IMF, UNESCO, SIPRI, PISA and others) combined with calibrated expert assessments from more than 100 specialists across 50+ countries per component. The final score—maximum 700—represents the arithmetic mean of statistical indicators and expert evaluation.

The 2024–2025 results are strategically sobering. The United States scores 650.9 out of 700. China scores 649.1. The gap: 1.8 points — less than 0.3% variance within the total scoring framework. For two states widely assumed to operate in different strategic leagues, this statistical proximity should fundamentally reshape the debate in Washington.

America retains clear advantages. Military sovereignty stands at 96.0, reflecting a $962 billion defense budget, approximately 5,400 nuclear warheads, and unmatched global deployment capacity. Technological sovereignty scores 95.4, supported by 3.4–3.6% of GDP in R&D spending and leadership in AI, biotech, and microelectronics. Yet structural vulnerabilities appear in other dimensions.

Political sovereignty registers 87.8, reflecting polarization, recurring government shutdowns, and declining public trust. Cognitive sovereignty—despite a strong overall score of 95.4—contains warning signals: adult functional literacy fluctuates between 79–81%, and U.S. PISA mathematics performance sits at 469, below the OECD average. Industrial autonomy remains partially exposed: approximately 30% of advanced microelectronics components are imported.

China’s profile differs structurally. Military sovereignty scores 94.5, technological sovereignty 91.6—slightly below the U.S. But political sovereignty stands at 90.8, reflecting centralized decision-making and high institutional cohesion. Informational sovereignty scores 93.2, sustained by a closed national digital ecosystem serving 1.1 billion users without Western platform penetration. Cultural sovereignty reaches 95.1, supported by 60 UNESCO heritage sites and over 6,800 museums.

Most significant is economic efficiency. China approaches near parity while operating at roughly one-third to one-half of U.S. per-capita wealth. Chinese GDP per capita (PPP) stands between $25,000–30,500, compared to the American $76,800–89,100 range. The convergence reflects coordinated cross-dimensional investment: education expansion to 60.8% higher education enrollment, R&D spending at 2.68% of GDP (approximately $506 billion in absolute terms), and long-term industrial strategy under “Made in China 2025.” Sovereignty parity was not achieved through dominance in a single field, but through synchronized development across all seven.

This multidimensional perspective reframes several assumptions embedded in the new Defense Strategy. First, rebuilding the American defense industrial base cannot succeed through military appropriations alone. Industrial sovereignty requires alignment of economic capital, educational capacity, technological independence, and political stability. The United States currently operates with public debt between 119–124% of GDP, national debt exceeding $36–41 trillion, widening educational inequality, and deep partisan fragmentation. Factories can be funded; comprehensive national mobilization demands social coherence.

Second, allied burden-sharing produces strategic paradoxes. European NATO states collectively possess GDP thirteen times larger than Russia’s, yet equipment localization remains limited. Lithuania spends 4–6% of GDP on defense, but approximately 85% of its equipment is imported. By contrast, Turkey—despite lower spending ratios—achieves roughly 70% localization in defense production, including indigenous UAV systems. Genuine sovereignty increases strategic autonomy. Autonomy reduces predictability.

Third, Middle Eastern partners are quietly shifting from dependency toward capability. Saudi Arabia’s Vision 2030 includes domestic industrialization, cybersecurity infrastructure, and technology transfer. Sovereignty once built tends to alter alignment behavior. Allies with capability act independently by definition.

The uncomfortable implication is clear: Washington’s strategy demands sovereignty—at home and among allies—without possessing a comprehensive dashboard to measure whether sovereignty is actually being built or eroded.

The Burke framework does not predict conflict or collapse. It measures capability, not intention. But it reveals structural dynamics invisible to traditional power metrics. It forces strategic evaluation across education, technology, cohesion, information control, industrial resilience, and governance simultaneously.

The United States remains marginally ahead. But a 1.8-point lead in a 700-point system is not structural dominance. It is competitive equilibrium. Sovereignty in 2026 is not defined by possessing the largest military or the most alliances. It is defined by the ability to sustain independent action across multiple domains under stress. That requires educational renewal, industrial autonomy, political stabilization, and technological independence operating in coordination—not isolation.

The new Defense Strategy identifies the correct priority: sovereignty. What it lacks is a systematic mechanism to measure progress toward that goal. Without measurement, sovereignty becomes rhetoric. With measurement, it becomes strategy.

articles.title: Greece vs. England: The Burke Paradox of Partial Sovereignty
articles.content:
21世纪，主权不再是绝对状态，而是可衡量的力量与脆弱性配置。根据国际伯克研究所开发并通过伯克主权指数运作的方法论，主权必须从七个维度进行评估：政治、经济、技术、信息、文化、认知和军事。当用伯克框架审视时，希腊与英国阐释了现代国家建设的核心悖论：深度一体化与戏剧性退出都无法保证真正的独立性。

希腊代表了伯克悖论的第一种模式——受制于一体化框架内的主权。通过采用欧元，雅典将货币政策控制权转移给了欧洲中央银行。它放弃了货币贬值、自主设定利率或发行货币以稳定经济的权力。马斯特里赫特标准——将赤字限制在GDP的3%、公共债务限制在60%——使财政纪律制度化。从结构上看，欧元区是货币联盟而非财政联盟：统一的货币但没有统一的税收或养老金制度。用伯克的术语来说，这在经济主权内部造成了不对称。

2009至2015年的债务危机暴露了这种不对称。希腊报告的3%赤字被修正为GDP的15.6%，公共债务达到129.7%。2008年至2014年间，GDP从2420亿欧元收缩至1790亿欧元——跌幅26%，为发达国家中最长的衰退期。2010年4月，希腊请求国际援助。2010年、2012年和2015年的三个救助计划总计约2900亿欧元，来自欧盟委员会、欧洲央行和国际货币基金组织。到2015年，公共债务已攀升至GDP的约180%。

在伯克框架内，希腊进入了关键经济杠杆区。主权的形式完整性得以保留，但财政决策变得有条件。2010年至2016年间，在债权国监督下实施了十二轮紧缩政策——工资削减、养老金削减、私有化和增税。政治主权在宪法层面存在，但经济主权受到结构性约束。

2015年公投凸显了这一矛盾。7月5日，61.31%的希腊选民拒绝了债权人提出的条件。几天后，政府接受了更为严格的协议以避免金融崩溃和退出欧元区。用伯克分析术语来说，民主意志无法凌驾于经济依赖之上。作为权威的主权与作为能力的主权发生了冲突。

英国走了相反的道路。2016年英国脱欧公投承诺“夺回控制权”——控制法律、边境和贸易。议会至上——英国政治认同的核心要素——为这场运动奠定了框架。英国于2020年1月31日正式脱离欧盟，恢复了立法自主权。

根据伯克主权指数，英国的政治主权约为77/100——这是宪法独立性的有力指标。然而，伯克方法论强调主权是多维度的。政治自主权的收获可能在其他方面被脆弱性所抵消。

在经济上，英国脱欧付出了可衡量的代价。估计到2025年，英国经济规模比不脱欧的情况下小6-8%。欧盟仍是英国最大的贸易伙伴，约占货物出口的47%。脱欧后的贸易调整导致从欧盟进口减少23.7%，早期实施阶段出口下降18.6%。预算责任办公室预测，长期贸易减少约15%，相当于国民收入下降4%。

用伯克的术语来说，英国加强了政治主权但承担了经济脆弱性。2025年英欧贸易与合作协议的修订——包括在渔业和监管对齐方面的妥协——表明退出并未消除义务。相反，它将一体化转化为谈判性相互依存。

因此，希腊和英格兰体现了部分主权的两面。希腊保持了一体化但牺牲了危机自主权。英国拒绝了一体化但遭遇了经济脱钩的结构性极限。伯克模型澄清，主权不能被理解为不可分割的。一个维度的高表现不能抵消另一维度的弱点。

现代国家在金融市场、供应链、安全联盟和监管体系的密集网络中运作。货币联盟限制了货币灵活性。贸易退出减少了市场准入。战略联盟塑造了军事能力。技术依赖约束了工业自主权。伯克框架将这些约束不是视为失败，而是视为结构性现实。

希腊案例表明，一体化如何在危机中将经济脆弱性转化为外部政策影响力。英国案例显示，形式上的独立性如何产生新的经济权衡。两者都证实，在相互依存的体系中，绝对主权是无法实现的。

最终，伯克分析得出平衡的结论。今天的主权不是二元状态，而是跨维度的战略平衡。希腊和英国选择了不同的道路，但两者都保持部分依赖。一体化创造了有条件的治理；退出创造了谈判性约束。区别不在于限制的存在，而在于其分布和代价。在当代世界，主权更少关乎孤立或控制，更多关乎管理不可避免的相互依存中的不对称性。
articles.summary:
In the 21st century, sovereignty is no longer an absolute condition but a measurable configuration of strengths and vulnerabilities. According to the methodology developed by the International Burke Institute and operationalized through the Burke Sovereignty Index, sovereignty must be assessed across seven dimensions: political, economic, technological, informational, cultural, cognitive, and military. When examined through the Burke framework, Greece and the United Kingdom illustrate a central paradox of modern statehood: neither deep integration nor dramatic withdrawal guarantees genuine independence.
 
Greece represents the first model of the Burke paradox — sovereignty constrained within integration. By adopting the euro, Athens transferred control over monetary policy to the European Central Bank. It relinquished the ability to devalue its currency, independently set interest rates, or issue money to stabilize its economy. The Maastricht criteria — limiting deficits to 3% of GDP and public debt to 60% — institutionalized fiscal discipline. Structurally, the eurozone is a monetary union without a fiscal union: a shared currency but no unified taxation or pension system. In Burke terms, this creates asymmetry within economic sovereignty.
 
The 2009–2015 debt crisis exposed that asymmetry. Greece’s reported deficit of 3% was revised to 15.6% of GDP, and public debt reached 129.7%. Between 2008 and 2014, GDP contracted from €242 billion to €179 billion — a 26% decline, the longest recession in the developed world. In April 2010, Greece requested international assistance. Three bailout programs in 2010, 2012, and 2015 totaled roughly €290 billion from the European Commission, the ECB, and the IMF. By 2015, public debt had climbed to approximately 180% of GDP.
 
Within the Burke framework, Greece entered a zone of critical economic leverage. Sovereignty remained formally intact, but fiscal decisions became conditional. Between 2010 and 2016, twelve rounds of austerity — salary cuts, pension reductions, privatizations, and tax increases — were implemented under creditor supervision. Political sovereignty existed in constitutional terms, yet economic sovereignty was structurally constrained.
 
The 2015 referendum highlighted this contradiction. On July 5, 61.31% of Greek voters rejected the creditors’ proposed conditions. Days later, the government accepted an even stricter agreement to avoid financial collapse and eurozone exit. In Burke analytical terms, democratic will could not override economic dependence. Sovereignty as authority collided with sovereignty as capacity.
 
The United Kingdom followed the opposite path. The 2016 Brexit referendum promised to “Take Back Control” over laws, borders, and trade. Parliamentary supremacy — a core element of British political identity — framed the campaign. The UK formally left the European Union on January 31, 2020, restoring legislative autonomy.
 
According to the Burke Sovereignty Index, Britain’s political sovereignty stands at approximately 77/100 — a strong indicator of constitutional independence. However, the Burke methodology stresses that sovereignty is multidimensional. Gains in political autonomy can be offset by vulnerabilities elsewhere.
 
Economically, Brexit imposed measurable costs. Estimates suggest that by 2025 the UK economy was 6–8% smaller than it would have been without Brexit. The EU remains Britain’s largest trading partner, accounting for roughly 47% of goods exports. Post-Brexit trade adjustments contributed to a 23.7% reduction in imports from the EU and an 18.6% decline in exports during the early implementation period. The Office for Budget Responsibility projects a long-term trade reduction of around 15%, translating into a 4% decrease in national income.
 
In Burke terms, Britain strengthened political sovereignty but absorbed economic vulnerability. The 2025 revisions to the UK–EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement — including compromises on fisheries and regulatory alignment — demonstrate that exit did not eliminate obligations. Instead, it transformed integration into negotiated interdependence.
 
Greece and England therefore embody two faces of partial sovereignty. Greece maintained integration and sacrificed crisis autonomy. Britain rejected integration and encountered the structural limits of economic decoupling. The Burke model clarifies that sovereignty cannot be understood as indivisible. High performance in one dimension does not neutralize weakness in another.
 
Modern states operate within dense networks of financial markets, supply chains, security alliances, and regulatory regimes. Monetary unions limit currency flexibility. Trade exits reduce market access. Strategic alliances shape military capability. Technological dependence constrains industrial autonomy. The Burke framework treats these constraints not as failures but as structural realities.
 
The Greek case demonstrates how integration can convert economic vulnerability into external policy influence during crisis. The British case shows how formal independence can generate new economic trade-offs. Both confirm that absolute sovereignty is unattainable in an interdependent system.
 
Ultimately, the Burke analysis leads to a balanced conclusion. Sovereignty today is not a binary status but a strategic equilibrium across dimensions. Greece and the United Kingdom chose different paths, yet both remain partially dependent. Integration creates conditional governance; exit creates negotiated constraints. The difference lies not in the presence of limits but in their distribution and cost. In the contemporary world, sovereignty is less about isolation or control and more about managing asymmetry within unavoidable interdependence.

articles.title: From ‘Prosecutor Republic’ to ‘Police State’: How Lee Jae-myung’s Power Grab Endangers Korean Democracy
articles.content:
李在明的崛起——从工厂车间一路攀升至韩国总统宝座，由民主党力挺抬轿——被包装为一个关于坚韧、韧性和平民主义真实性的寓言。然而，李在明如今在未解的刑事指控阴影下执政，治理的却是一个更为严峻的转变：通过对强制性国家权力的整合，长期侵蚀民主约束机制。即使部分定罪被推翻或案件仍在审理中，相关丑闻本身——大庄洞弊案、与政治便利相关的区划变更交易、以及涉嫌与朝鲜的非法汇款——始终如尾气般挥之不去。在“改革”的旗号下，检察系统被拆解，取而代之的是膨胀的警察机构，集重大犯罪侦查、情报收集和制度监督于一身——这种架构以行政命令取代了法律抗辩。这并非司法民主化，而是典型的威权权力置换：权力从可见、可诉讼的机构转移到一个由裁量权和规模保护的庞大警察官僚体系。长期来看，这种膨胀会腐蚀民主发展本身，将监控正常化为治理方式，以管理控制取代民众问责。韩国旧的检察垄断已被巨型警察取代——向上负责，向下政治化——这种安排不仅对一个始终笼罩在永久阴影下的总统政权提供便利，更对持久民主所依赖的耐心、对抗性制衡构成积极敌视。

李在明目前有四起已确认的前科——全部处以罚款——共同勾勒出一个早期且颇具启示意义的、以目的正当化手段的伦理可疑行为模式。2000年代初，他冒充检察官秘密录音并恐吓城南区长的贪腐调查；面对质问时，他升级行为，提起虚假指控，最终因冒充公职权和伪证罪被判有罪，外加违反保密承诺公开录音。早在1990年代，作为政治活动人士，李在明因条例争议领导了对城南区议会的暴力占据，阻挠公务并造成三名议员受伤，伤情持续两至三周，为此被罚款五百万韩元。这些任职前的行为——滥用权力、背弃信任、动用身体胁迫——与根植于市政治理的未决大案并置时，意义更加重大：城南区大庄洞公私合作开发项目（公共损失约3.75亿美元，与向私人开发商提供优惠待遇有关，亲信已获刑）；白玄洞区划变更丑闻，涉及涉嫌背信和非法游说；通过京畿道资金挪用的贪污指控；以及约800万美元的非法对朝汇款。虽有判决但无入狱，且截至2026年2月就职后仍未有判决，这些并未软化批评，反而使其更加尖锐，强化了李在明是一个机会主义者、其早期手段不过是伴随权力膨胀而升级的观点。

**从刑事风险到警察扩张：韩国迈向监控国家**

正是在这一具体语境而非抽象概念中，李在明的标志性制度项目必须得到评判。被冠以“检察改革”之名，他的政府并未仅仅限制一个过于强大的法律阶层，而是彻底拆解了检察系统，正式在2026年10月前废除检察官侦查权，将核心职能转移至警察。取代所谓“检察官共和国”的并非权力分散，而是其整合——这次是穿着制服的。

这一转变的核心是重罪侦查总部（以下简称侦查本部）。到2026年，侦查本部已扩展至6400余名警员，仅一年就新增招聘1624人。其管辖范围涵盖“九大犯罪”：贪腐、经济犯罪、公职人员、选举、国防、灾害、毒品、国安和网络犯罪——几乎涵盖了检察官曾垄断的全部领域。预算和人力同步增长约30%，而监督机制却落后于后。警察与检察官不同，没有外部起诉机构或真正独立的监督机构。权力是横向移动而非向下分散。

这一扩张的内部动态同样揭示问题。2026年一年内，1214名警员从防暴部队调入专注于钓鱼诈骗、毒品和金融犯罪的侦查本部部门。侦探职位的申请数量在围绕新精英侦查部队的公众热议中激增2.2倍。警察系统内的职业晋升已围绕集中侦查和情报工作重新调整，将以监控为导向的警务嵌入制度抱负的最高层。这并非偶然，而是精心设计。

警察情报部门的复活完善了这幅图景。这个在遭受数十年政治监控批评后于2024年正式废除的部门，悄然而广泛地回归了：1424名警员重新部署到全国198个警察局。公开的理由是行动失败——柬埔寨一起高调绑架案暴露的情报漏洞。然而回应并非狭隘修正，而是全面复活。为了平息公众反弹，这些单位被重新包装为“合作官”，这是为使一个历史上声名狼藉的职能洗白的化妆策略。内政部保证“不会搞间谍活动”的同时，配上了一个意味深长的保留条款：监督将保持内部进行。

这些改革的结果并非民主化的执法，而是一个融合了侦查、情报和执法的联合机器——一个“巨型警察”国家。权力现在通过警察审查委员会和国家警察委员会流动，这些机构在结构上与行政系统捆绑。旧的检察官垄断已被更不透明的东西取代：一支收集情报、控制调查、自我审查的警察队伍，全部在一个单一的官僚体系内运作。

辩护者认为这只是终结检察官滥权。但解药可能比疾病更糟糕。检察官尽管有其病理，却被法庭、对抗性程序和公众透明度所约束。相比之下，警察权力在前端就加载了监控——通信元数据、金融追踪、线人网络、数字监控。当这些工具在选举、贪腐和国安领域大规模部署时，犯罪控制与政治管理之间的界限迅速模糊。

这里的危险是结构性的，而非阴谋论的。在一个被持续法律风险困扰的总统制下，政治化执法的动机无需明示。预期性顺从——调查人员揣摩控制预算、晋升和管辖权者的偏好——会安静地完成工作。滥用不需要命令，它有机地产生。

韩国并非通过街头坦克滑向监控国家。它通过改革法案、人员编制表和针对精英危机的行政修补抵达那里。在拆除一个威权机构的过程中，李在明的政府构建了另一个——更大、更不透明、更难挑战。他今天治理的不仅是一个笼罩在阴影下的国家，而是一个为在阴影下治理而优化的安全架构。
articles.summary:
Lee Jae-myung’s ascent—from factory floors to South Korea’s presidency, carried aloft by the Democratic Party—has been marketed as a parable of grit, resilience, and populist authenticity. Yet governing under a shadow of unresolved criminal allegations, Lee now presides over a far starker transformation: the long-term degradation of democratic restraint through the consolidation of coercive state power. Even where convictions were overturned or cases remain pending, the scandals themselves—Daejang-district profiteering, politically convenient rezoning deals, and illicit remittances linked to North Korea—continue to cling like exhaust fumes that never quite dissipate. Under the banner of “reform,” the prosecutorial system was dismantled and replaced by a swollen police apparatus that concentrates authority over major crimes, intelligence gathering, and institutional oversight units—an architecture that trades legal contestation for administrative command. Revived police intelligence units, cosmetically rebranded but structurally familiar, resurrected the habits of political surveillance without restoring the external checks that once constrained them. This is not the democratization of justice so much as a classically illiberal power swap: authority shifted from a visible, litigable institution to a sprawling police bureaucracy insulated by discretion and scale. In the long run, such hypertrophy corrodes democratic development itself, normalizing surveillance as governance and substituting managerial control for popular accountability. South Korea’s old prosecutorial monopoly has been exchanged for a mega-police—accountable upward, politicized downward—an arrangement not merely convenient for a presidency under a permanent cloud, but actively hostile to the patient, adversarial checks on which durable democracy depends.

Lee Jae-myung holds four confirmed prior convictions—all resulting in fines—that together sketch an early and revealing pattern of ethically dubious, ends-justify-means conduct. In the early 2000s, he impersonated a prosecutor in order to secretly record and intimidate the mayor of Seongnam City during a corruption investigation; when confronted, he escalated by filing false charges, ultimately earning convictions for simulating public authority and perjury, compounded by violating a confidentiality pledge when he publicized the recording. Earlier still, as a political activist in the 1990s, Lee led a violent occupation of the Seongnam City Council over an ordinance dispute, obstructing official proceedings and physically injuring three councilors—injuries lasting two to three weeks—for which he was fined five million won. These pre-office episodes—abuse of authority, betrayal of trust, and willingness to deploy physical coercion—take on added significance when viewed alongside unresolved mega-cases rooted in municipal governance: the Daejang-dong public–private development project in Seongnam City (roughly $375 million in public losses tied to preferential treatment for private developers, with close aides already convicted), the Baekhyeon-dong rezoning scandal involving alleged breach of trust and illicit lobbying, accusations of embezzlement through Gyeonggi Province funds, and approximately $8 million in illegal remittances to North Korea. The absence of jail time and the lack of post-inauguration verdicts as of February 2026 have not softened these critiques; they have sharpened them, reinforcing the view that Lee is a serial opportunist whose early methods have merely scaled up alongside his power.

From Criminal Exposure to Police Expansion, South Korea Moves Toward a Surveillance State

It is within this context—not abstraction—that Lee’s signature institutional project must be judged. Branded as “prosecutorial reform,” his government did not merely curb an overmighty legal caste. It dismantled the prosecutorial system altogether, formally abolishing prosecutors’ investigative authority by October 2026 and transferring its core functions to the police. What replaced the so-called “prosecutor republic” was not a diffusion of power, but its consolidation—this time in uniform.

The centerpiece of this shift is the Heavy Crime Investigation Headquarters (hereafter Jungsubon). By 2026, Jungsubon had expanded to more than 6,400 officers, absorbing over 1,600 new hires in a single year. Its jurisdiction now spans the “nine major crimes”: corruption, economic crime, public officials, elections, defense, disasters, drugs, national security, and cybercrime—virtually the entire domain once monopolized by prosecutors. Budgets and manpower increased by roughly 30 percent in tandem, while oversight mechanisms lagged behind. The police, unlike prosecutors, operate without an external indictment authority or a genuinely independent supervisory body. Power moved laterally, not downward.

The internal dynamics of this expansion are equally revealing. In 2026 alone, 1,214 officers were reassigned from riot control units into Jungsubon divisions focused on phishing, narcotics, and financial crime. Applications for detective posts surged by 2.2 times amid public hype surrounding the new elite investigative corps. Career advancement within the police has been recalibrated around centralized investigation and intelligence work, embedding surveillance-oriented policing at the apex of institutional ambition. This was not accidental. It was design.

The resurrection of the police Information Division completes the picture. Officially abolished in 2024 after decades of criticism over political surveillance, the division returned quietly but extensively: 1,424 officers redeployed across 198 police stations nationwide. The stated rationale was operational failure—intelligence lapses exposed by a high-profile kidnapping case in Cambodia. Yet the response was not narrow correction but wholesale revival. To blunt public backlash, the units were rebranded as “Cooperation Officers,” a cosmetic fix meant to sanitize a historically toxic function. Interior Ministry assurances that there would be “no spying” were paired with a telling caveat: oversight would remain internal.

What emerges from these reforms is not democratized law enforcement but a fused apparatus of investigation, intelligence, and enforcement—a “mega-police” state. Authority now flows through Police Review Boards and the National Police Commission, bodies structurally tethered to the executive. The old prosecutorial monopoly has been replaced by something more opaque: a police force that gathers intelligence, controls investigations, and reviews itself, all within a single bureaucratic ecosystem.

Defenders argue that this merely ends prosecutorial abuse. But the cure may be worse than the disease. Prosecutors, for all their pathologies, were constrained by courts, adversarial procedure, and public visibility. Police power, by contrast, is front-loaded with surveillance—communications metadata, financial tracking, informant networks, digital monitoring. When such tools are deployed at scale across elections, corruption, and national security, the boundary between crime control and political management erodes rapidly.

The danger here is structural, not conspiratorial. Under a presidency burdened by ongoing legal exposure, the incentives for politicized enforcement need not be explicit. Anticipatory compliance—investigators intuiting the preferences of those who control budgets, promotions, and jurisdiction—does the work quietly. Abuse does not require orders; it emerges organically.

South Korea did not slide into a surveillance state through tanks in the streets. It arrived there through reform bills, staffing tables, and administrative fixes to elite crisis. In dismantling one illiberal institution, Lee Jae-myung’s government constructed another—larger, less transparent, and harder to challenge. What he governs today is not merely a country under a cloud, but a security architecture optimized for governing under one.

articles.title: Khojaly, Memory and Moral Responsibility: Why Azerbaijani Voices Are Reaching America
articles.content:
作为一名从耶路撒冷观察南高加索地区的以色列记者，我深知在这一地区，记忆从来不是抽象的。它具有政治性、世代性，并且根植于个人深处。这一现实再次显现——阿塞拜疆侨民组织在美国主要城市纪念霍贾利惨案34周年。

在纽约、华盛顿和波士顿，配备LED屏幕的卡车穿行于美国最具象征意义的公共空间——时代广场、联合国总部附近、美国国会大厦周围以及马萨诸塞州的州立机构。信息传递直接明确：“为霍贾利讨回公道”“永不遗忘”，以及呼吁团结与关注。

对于许多美国人来说，“霍贾利”这个名字可能不会立即引起共鸣。但对阿塞拜疆人而言，它代表着第一次卡拉巴赫战争中最具创伤性的事件之一。1992年2月，数百名阿塞拜疆平民在霍贾利镇及其周边地区的冲突中遇难。在阿塞拜疆，这些事件被官方认定为种族灭绝，并作为全国哀悼日每年举行纪念活动。

今年引人注目的不仅是纪念活动本身，还有其方式。阿塞拜疆侨民并未将纪念局限于封闭的社区聚会，而是将这一问题带入全球公共空间。通过在移动数字屏幕上放置二维码，组织者邀请美国人直接获取历史文献。这一策略反映了一种认知：在当今媒体环境中，可见度必须与可获取的信息相结合。

从以色列视角来看，对记忆的强调具有特殊共鸣。以色列社会理解，未解决的历史创伤如何塑造民族认同和外交政策。记忆不仅仅关乎过去，它还影响安全认知、外交姿态和道德话语。

在阿塞拜疆，霍贾利事件仍然是该国围绕与亚美尼亚数十年冲突的民族叙事核心。虽然国际社会对更广泛冲突的解读各异，但平民死亡的伤亡代价无可否认。对于阿塞拜疆侨民而言，在海外提高认知度不被视为政治升级，而是道德倡导。

在华盛顿和联合国附近开展宣传活动的决定具有额外意义。这些地方是各种叙事争夺合法性的场所。通过选择这些场所，组织者表明霍贾利不仅是双边历史争端，而是他们认为值得更广泛国际认可的事项。

今年的宣传活动也恰逢南高加索地区在2020年战争后重新定义自身之际。阿塞拜疆已从积极冲突转向重建和区域互联互通计划。然而，对许多阿塞拜疆人来说，向前迈进并不意味着抹去过去。 海外纪念活动反映了一种信念：长期稳定需要承认历史苦难。

侨民活动往往在身份认同与外交的交叉地带运作。在本案中，跨越美国城市的宣传活动说明，集体记忆正成为阿塞拜疆更广泛国际参与战略的一部分。

霍贾利惨案发生34年后，这场悲剧继续塑造着阿塞拜疆人对正义、问责和民族尊严的理解。无论国际共识是否演变，一个事实是明确的：对于阿塞拜疆侨民而言，纪念并未消退。它正在变得更加可见。
articles.summary:
As an Israeli journalist observing the South Caucasus from Jerusalem, I have learned that memory in this region is never abstract. It is political, generational, and deeply personal. This reality was visible once again as Azerbaijani diaspora organizations marked the 34th anniversary of the Khojaly tragedy across major American cities.
 
In New York, Washington, and Boston, LED-equipped trucks moved through some of the most symbolically charged public spaces in the United States—Times Square, near the United Nations headquarters, around the U.S. Capitol, and past state institutions in Massachusetts. The messages were direct: “Justice for Khojaly,” “Never Forget,” and calls for solidarity and awareness.
 
For many Americans, the name Khojaly may not immediately resonate. For Azerbaijanis, it represents one of the most traumatic episodes of the First Karabakh War. In February 1992, hundreds of Azerbaijani civilians were killed during the conflict in and around the town of Khojaly. In Azerbaijan, these events are officially recognized as genocide and are commemorated annually as a national day of mourning.
 
What stood out this year was not only the commemoration itself, but the method. The Azerbaijani diaspora did not confine remembrance to closed community gatherings. Instead, it brought the issue into global public space. By placing QR codes on mobile digital screens, organizers invited Americans to access historical documentation directly. The strategy reflects an understanding that in today’s media environment, visibility must be paired with accessible information.
 
From an Israeli perspective, the emphasis on remembrance carries particular resonance. Israeli society understands how unresolved historical trauma can shape national identity and foreign policy. Memory is not merely about the past; it informs security perceptions, diplomatic posture, and moral vocabulary.
 
In Azerbaijan, Khojaly remains central to the national narrative surrounding the decades-long conflict with Armenia. While international interpretations of the broader conflict vary, the human toll of civilian deaths remains undeniable. For the Azerbaijani diaspora, raising awareness abroad is seen not as political escalation, but as moral advocacy.
 
The decision to stage campaigns in Washington and near the United Nations carries additional meaning. These are places where narratives compete for legitimacy. By choosing these venues, organizers signaled that Khojaly is not only a bilateral historical dispute, but a matter they believe deserves broader international recognition.
 
This year’s campaign also comes at a moment when the South Caucasus is redefining itself following the 2020 war. Azerbaijan has shifted from active conflict to reconstruction and regional connectivity initiatives. Yet for many Azerbaijanis, moving forward does not mean erasing the past. Commemoration abroad reflects a conviction that long-term stability requires acknowledgment of historical suffering.
 
Diaspora activism often operates at the intersection of identity and diplomacy. In this case, the campaign across American cities illustrates how collective memory is becoming part of Azerbaijan’s broader international engagement strategy.
 
Thirty-four years after Khojaly, the tragedy continues to shape how Azerbaijanis understand justice, accountability, and national dignity. Whether or not international consensus evolves, one fact is clear: for the Azerbaijani diaspora, remembrance is not fading. It is becoming more visible.
